articles.title: Solos And Small Law Firms: A Market Ripe For Disruption?
articles.content:
汤森路透最近的一项调查和报告为中型律师事务所描绘了一幅相当黯淡的图景。而法律实务管理平台Clio的类似调查则提供了更加严峻的画面，显示独立律师和小型律师事务所面临的形势更加严峻。但威胁之中也蕴含着机遇。问题始终在于：谁将抓住这些机遇。

如果独立律师和小型事务所不做出改变，两者都将面临新的、更强大的竞争。两者都处于被颠覆的成熟时机。

他们正在使用人工智能，但……

Clio在5月份发布的报告揭示了一些矛盾的发现。一方面，报告显示约71%的独立律师和75%的小型律师事务所正在使用人工智能。这令人鼓舞。

但这些事务所中超过一半没有关于人工智能使用的任何政策。这反过来又带来了可能导致财务损失甚至毁灭的风险。对于独立律师来说，这也许并不令人意外，因为指南往往只存在于独立律师的脑海中。但即便如此，独立律师事务所也有员工需要了解人工智能风险和适当使用。

除此之外，也许与此相关的是，只有38%的独立律师和小型事务所鼓励在既定框架内使用人工智能，这使他们面临来自有此做法的事务所的竞争风险。

另一个有趣的发现是，使用人工智能的人中近一半使用的是ChatGPT或Copilot等通用平台。Clio在报告中指出，这些工具并非为法律工作而设计，会带来额外风险。我不完全认同这一点，因为小型事务所和独立律师所做的法律工作可能不需要昂贵的企业级平台所提供的同等级别保护。独立律师和小型事务所更多地专注于个人和小企业事务、家庭法、简单信托和遗产，甚至刑事业务以维持运营。模糊客户身份并提出通用问题以获取所需信息更容易。但仍然存在人工智能工具提供不准确信息的风险。

因此，综合来看，律师使用人工智能的方式可能正在创造威胁利润乃至生存的风险。但这还不是最糟糕的。

使用人工智能但未产生利润

按小时计费的律师事务所面临一个基本的经济事实。正如Clio报告中敏锐指出的那样：“人工智能创造了一个悖论。你越高效，计费越少。在研究、起草和行政工作上节省的时间不会自动转化为收入，除非这些增加的能力被新工作填满。”

或者通过使用固定费用或按任务或价值计费等方式对工作进行不同收费。同样，Clio说得很好：“如果人工智能帮助你在一个小时内完成原本需要五个小时的可计费任务，你实际上给了客户80%的折扣。”尽管Clio发现71%的客户实际上更喜欢固定费用。

但Clio还发现了其他情况：86%的独立律师事务所和78%的小型律师事务所没有调整定价模式以考虑人工智能。只有约三分之一通过使用人工智能增加了收入。近四分之一表示人工智能对他们的收入没有影响。一切如常。

所以，好消息是独立律师和小型事务所正在使用人工智能。坏消息是他们不一定安全或有效地使用它。更糟糕的消息是，他们没有有利可图地使用它。

机遇与威胁

像人工智能这样的节省时间的工具开辟了重大机遇。但前提是那些节省的时间被有效利用来做诸如加强业务和客户开发之类的事情。这些事情能带来更多收入。

此外，人工智能为以前无法盈利完成的工作提供了盈利完成的机会。毫无疑问，有一个巨大的服务不足的法律市场可以通过正确的方法开拓。

这就引向了小型事务所和独立律师重新思考计费模式的绝对必要性。人工智能为进入这些新市场创造了条件，但只有当事务所放弃对按小时计费模式的依赖时才行。使用人工智能，按小时计费模式一方面将许多潜在客户排除在法律服务之外，另一方面导致律师利润下降。

所有这些都指向了尽快寻找不同模式的必要性。无疑会有人想明白并付诸行动。人们只需看看Morgan and Morgan这样的事务所，就能看到思维方式巨变所能带来的颠覆力量。

Morgan and Morgan最初是佛罗里达州的一家小型律师事务所。但其领导层看到了利用激进广告和建立面向消费者的全国性品牌的机会。它颠覆了原告方风险代理费市场。

某处的某个人将看到利用人工智能和定价进行类似颠覆的机会。特别是因为大多数潜在客户渴望一种不基于时间的计费模式。

而且，不一定非要由传统律师事务所模式来完成这件事。想想H&R Block如何颠覆了税务申报准备模式。或者TurboTax如何颠覆了H&R Block。关键是，重新思考独立律师和小型事务所如何提供服务并确定价值，可能导致一些大的赢家和输家。

当然，汤森路透和Clio都在销售人工智能实务管理工具，因此你必须对他们的部分调查结果持保留态度。但很明显，独立律师和小型法律市场并没有充分利用人工智能带来的机遇。这意味着它们可能正处于被颠覆的成熟时机，就像Morgan and Morgan对原告业务带来的颠覆，以及H&R Block和TurboTax对税务准备带来的颠覆一样。

你有一个市场在很大程度上忽视了一大群有法律需求的人。你的大多数消费者和潜在消费者不喜欢你的计费模式。而你拥有可以接触那个市场并满足这些需求和愿望的技术。某个人会抓住这个机会。这以前发生过。还会再次发生。

Stephen Embry是一位律师、演讲者、博主和作家。他发布TechLaw Crossroads，这是一个致力于审视技术、法律与法律执业之间紧张关系的博客。

这篇文章《独立律师和小型律师事务所：一个等待被颠覆的市场？》首次出现在Above the Law。
articles.summary:
A recent survey and report from Thomson Reuters painted a rather bleak picture for midsize law firms. A similar survey, this time from the practice management platform, Clio, provides an even more dire picture facing solo and small law firms. But where there are threats, there are also opportunities. As always, the issue is who will capitalize on those opportunities.

And if solos and small firms don’t change, both will face new and more formidable competition. Both are ripe for disruption.

They’re Using AI, But…

The Clio report, which came out in May, reveals some paradoxical findings. On the one hand, it shows some 71% of solos and 75% of small law firms are using AI. That’s encouraging.

But more than half of these firms don’t have any policy about the use of AI. This, in turn, creates risks that could lead to financial hits and even ruin. For solos, that’s perhaps not all that surprising since the guidelines are often kept in the solo lawyer’s head. But still, even solo law firms have staffs that need to be aware of AI risks and appropriate use.

Beyond this, and perhaps consistent with it, only 38% of solos and small firms encourage the use of AI within a defined framework, putting them at risk of competition from firms that do.

Another interesting finding is that almost half of those using AI are using generic platforms like ChatGPT or Copilot. Clio makes the point in its report that these tools aren’t made for legal work and create additional risks. I’m not sure I buy this completely since the type of legal work done by small firms and solos may not require the same level of protection provided by expensive enterprise platforms. Solos and small firms focus more on individual and small business matters, family law, simple trusts and estate and even criminal work to keep going. It’s easier to fuzz up client identity and ask generic questions to get the information needed. But there is still the risk that the AI tool will provide inaccurate information.

So putting this together, you have lawyers using AI in ways that may be creating risks that threaten profits and even survival if things go bad. But that’s not the worst of it.

Using AI But Not for Profit

There is a fundamental economic fact facing law firms that bill by the hour. As perceptively stated in the Clio report, “AI creates a paradox. The more efficient you become, the less you bill. Time saved on research, drafting, and administration doesn’t automatically translate into revenue unless that added capacity gets filled with new work.”

Or the work gets billed differently through the use of things like flat fees or task- or value-based billing. Again, Clio puts it well: “If AI helps you complete a billable task in one hour instead of five, you’ve effectively handed your client an 80% discount.” This despite the fact that Clio found 71% of clients actually prefer flat fees.

But here’s what else Clio found: 86% of solo firms and 78% of small firms have not adjusted their pricing models to account for AI. Only about a third have increased their revenues with the use of AI. Almost a quarter say AI has had no impact on their revenue. It’s business as usual.

So, the good news is solos and small firms are using AI. The bad news is they aren’t necessarily using it safely or effectively. The even worse news is that they aren’t using it profitably.

An Opportunity and Threat

Tools that save time as AI does, open up a significant opportunity. But only if that saved time is used effectively to do things like enhance business and client development. Things that lead to more revenues.

Moreover, AI opens up the chance to do work profitably that before AI could not be done in profitable ways. And no question, there is a vast underserved legal market that could be tapped into with the right approach.

Which leads to the absolute necessity for small firms and solos to rethink their billing models. AI enables access to these new markets but only if firms drop the reliance on the billable hour model. With AI, the billable hour model prices many would-be clients out of legal services, on the one hand, and leads to a drop of profits for the lawyers, on the other.

All of which points to the need to find different models. And soon. There will no doubt be those who figure this out and run with it. One only need look at a firm like Morgan and Morgan to see the power of disruption a sea change in thinking can bring.

Morgan and Morgan was founded as a small law firm in Florida. But its leadership saw an opportunity to capitalize on aggressive advertising and to build a consumer-facing national brand. It disrupted the plaintiffs’ contingency fee market.

Someone somewhere will see similar opportunities for disruption with AI and pricing. Especially since most potential clients are hungry for a billing model not based on time spent.

And it won’t necessarily be the traditional law firm model that does it. Think H&R Block that disrupted the tax return preparation model. Or TurboTax that disrupted H&R Block. The point is that rethinking how solos and small firms provide their services and determine value could lead to some big winners and losers.

Certainly, Thomson Reuters and Clio are selling AI practice management tools and hence, you have to take with a grain of salt some of their survey results. But it’s pretty clear that the solo and small law market is not capitalizing on the opportunities AI holds. That means they may be ripe for the same kind of disruption Morgan and Morgan brought for the plaintiffs’ practice and H&R Block and TurboTax brought to tax preparation.

You have a market ignoring in large part a huge group of those with legal needs. You have a billing model most of your consumers and potential consumers don’t like. And you have technology that makes it possible to reach that market and serve those needs and wants. Someone will pounce. It has happened before. It will happen again.

Stephen Embry is a lawyer, speaker, blogger, and writer. He publishes TechLaw Crossroads, a blog devoted to the examination of the tension between technology, the law, and the practice of law.

The post Solos And Small Law Firms: A Market Ripe For Disruption? appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Medicine’s Monopoly Problem
articles.content:
塔希尔·阿明最初想踢职业足球，后来却进入了法律行业。20世纪90年代初，他开始专攻知识产权法，当时这一领域还很新颖，颇具前沿性。在英美两国从事了十年商业知识产权实务后，他于2004年前往印度，想了解新的全球知识产权规则对那些从未被纳入考量的人造成了什么影响。当时正值印度被要求遵守WTO专利规则之际，这些规则将影响每年费用高达数万美元的HIV药物可及性。这段经历促使他在2006年共同创立了I-MAC。

在这场对话中，塔希尔深入剖析了药品专利的政治经济学：20世纪70年代末以来的新自由主义政策如何将公共资助的研究拱手让给私营公司；这些公司如何构建庞大的专利组合来推迟仿制药竞争；以及“创新”这个词如何成为抵御任何真正审视的盾牌。他还谈到了I-MAC的工作成就、面临的反弹，以及为何认为自己是一个“愤世嫉俗的乐观主义者”，相信长远来看这是一场值得坚持的游戏。

这篇文章首次发布于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Tahir Amin started out wanting to play professional football in England. He found law instead, and specifically intellectual property law in the early 1990s, when the field was still new enough to feel cutting edge. After a decade of commercial IP practice in the UK and the US, he moved to India in 2004 to understand what the new global IP rules were doing to the people they never accounted for. He arrived just as India was being required to comply with WTO patent rules that would affect access to HIV medicines costing tens of thousands of dollars a year. That experience led him to co-found I-MAC in 2006.

In this conversation, Tahir walks through the political economy of drug patents: how neoliberal policy from the late 1970s onward handed control of publicly funded research to private companies, how those companies built out sprawling patent portfolios to delay generic competition, and how the word “innovation” became a shield against any real scrutiny. He also talks about what I-MAC’s work has achieved, the blowback it has faced, and why he considers himself a “cynical optimist” who believes the long game is the only game worth playing.

  • Patent stacking is the norm, not the exception. Novo Nordisk filed more than 320 patent applications around the single active ingredient in the Ozempic family of drugs.
  • 1 in 3 Americans are skipping doses or cutting pills to afford their medications. This is a policy problem rooted in decisions made over the past four decades.
  • The word “innovation” replaced “invention” deliberately around 1976–77, allowing corporations to privatize public-funded knowledge and charge rent for it.
  • I-MAC’s public patent database puts the evidence on the table. The pharma industry attacks the messenger rather than engaging the data.
  • Incrementalism is the system’s release valve. Real change requires playing the long game, not chasing the next quick win.

The post Medicine’s Monopoly Problem appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Breyer Pushes Back On Claims That Politics Rules The Supreme Court
articles.content:
编辑按：欢迎阅读我们的每日专栏——每日名言。

在任命新大法官时，政治因素确实存在；世界上的每个政治团体都会为自己的首选候选人进行游说。但一旦法官被任命，情况就不同了。法官思考的是，根据法律，什么是正确的判决？

——退休大法官斯蒂芬·布雷耶，在接受Bloomberg Law深度访谈时发表的评论。访谈涉及过去几年美国司法系统“信任度下降”的现象。布雷耶表示，他认为这种下降并非源于最高法院的政治化，但他随后承认，如果从非常长远的时间来看，信任的丧失可能构成威胁。

斯蒂西·扎雷夫斯基是Above the Law的执行主编，自2011年起在该网站工作。她非常欢迎收到您的来信，请随时通过电子邮件与她联系，无论是提供线索、问题、评论还是批评。您可以在Bluesky、X/Twitter和Threads上关注她，或在LinkedIn上与她联系。

这篇文章《布雷耶反驳政治主导最高法院的说法》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. note: Welcome to our daily feature, Quote of the Day.

It’s politics when you’re appointing a new justice; every political group in the world [campaigns for their preferred nominee.] But once the judge is appointed, it’s different. The judge is thinking, what is the correct decision according to the law?

— Retired Justice Stephen Breyer, in comments given during a wide-ranging interview with Bloomberg Law, concerning a sense of “declining trust” in the American judiciary over the course of the last few years. Breyer said he didn’t think the decline was due to the Supreme Court’s politics, but went on to admit that the loss of trust could be a threat “if you’re talking very long run.”

Staci Zaretsky is the managing editor of Above the Law, where she’s worked since 2011. She’d love to hear from you, so please feel free to email her with any tips, questions, comments, or critiques. You can follow her on Bluesky, X/Twitter, and Threads, or connect with her on LinkedIn.

The post Breyer Pushes Back On Claims That Politics Rules The Supreme Court appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: New Poll Shows Americans — Even Republicans — Think The Supreme Court Needs To Be Reined In
articles.content:
维拉赛特本周发布的一项新民调结果，应该让那些假装最高法院合法性危机只是党派一厢情愿的人警醒。简而言之：危机确实存在。

多年来我们一直在追踪最高法院的合法性危机，数据持续恶化。表示对最高法院“非常有信心”或“相当有信心”的登记选民比例已降至22%。有些慢性病的民意支持率都比这高。维拉赛特的调查为这一图景增添了新的细节……而这些细节对最高法院并不友好。

数据显示，64%的民主党人和47%的共和党人认为，鉴于最高法院与党派政治的纠葛，美国需要更强的机制来约束法院的行为。近半数共和党人！在两党目前几乎在任何问题上都无法达成一致的背景下，超过四成支持总统的选民认为法院需要约束机制。

最高法院被视为政治机构当然不是新闻。正如卡根大法官在2022年的一次演讲中所说，当法官“踏入看起来像是政治过程延伸的领域时”，他们就为自己制造了合法性问题。维拉赛特的民调表明，公众比最高法院本身更深刻地吸收了这一教训。早在2024年我们就指出，83%的受访者表示党派性至少在某些时候影响着最高法院的裁决。

只有31%的共和党人——以及仅7%的民主党人——告诉维拉赛特，他们认为最高法院“现在的样子就很好”。也就是说，执政党选出的法官占当前绝对多数的政党中，约十分之三的党员支持这个法院，而反对党的成员中仅有约十四分之一持此观点。这就是制度崩溃在民调中的表现形式。去年我们注意到，67%的共和党人和26%的民主党人对最高法院持好感，即使那些数字也显示这是一个深陷困境的机构。此后，情况继续恶化。

这一切并非凭空发生。Strength In Numbers与维拉赛特今年早些时候的另一项民调发现，65%的美国人支持对大法官实行18年任期限制，包括61%的独立人士和56%的共和党人。ATL的乔·帕特里斯多年来一直在倡导任期限制，他最近的论述阐明了为什么这代表了目前最必要且最不危险的改革方案。他的核心论点是，终身任期或许曾经是使最高法院免受政治影响的盾牌，但现在它已变成一把利剑，正在摧毁最高法院的合法性。改革势头正在增强：乔指出，反对改革的力量开始感到紧张，这表明变革正在真正发生。

听好了，美国人——包括大量共和党人——看到一个已成为政治机构的法院，他们认为这不可接受，他们希望采取行动。维拉赛特的民调为已有的大量记录增添了新的证据，这些记录自乔在它尚未成为潮流之前就一直在追踪，表明改革并非边缘需求，而是对一个显而易见问题的显而易见回应。

早些时候：《经历多年“看我表演”时刻后，最高法院信任度跌至新低》

《唐纳德·特朗普希望所有人（除了他自己）尊重最高法院的合法性》

《美国人知道最高法院的真相》

《福克斯新闻展示美国人有多讨厌最高法院》

《最高法院的支持率当然在下滑》

《最高法院不断下降的声望》

凯瑟琳·鲁比诺是Above the Law的高级编辑、The Jabot播客主持人，以及Thinking Like A Lawyer的联合主持人。ATL的线人是最好的，请与她联系。欢迎通过电子邮件发送任何线索、问题或评论，并在Twitter上关注她@Kathryn1或在Bluesky上关注她@kathryn1。
articles.summary:
A new poll from Verasight dropped this week, and its findings should be a wake-up call for everyone currently pretending the Supreme Court’s legitimacy crisis is a partisan fever dream. Short version: it isn’t.

We’ve been tracking the Court’s legitimacy crisis for years now, and the numbers keep getting grimmer. Registered voters with “a great deal” or “quite a bit” of faith in the Supreme Court is down to a combined 22 percent. There are chronic diseases that poll better. The Verasight survey adds some new texture to that picture… and it is not flattering to the Court.

The data shows that 64% of Democrats and 47% of Republicans agree the United States needs stronger mechanisms for controlling the Court’s actions given its entanglement with partisan politics. Nearly half of Republicans! On a topic where the two parties currently agree on roughly nothing, you’ve got a near-majority of the president’s own voters saying the Court needs guardrails.

The Court being seen as a political institution is, of course, not news. As Justice Kagan put it in a 2022 speech, judges create legitimacy problems for themselves “when they stray into places where it looks like they’re an extension of the political process.” The Verasight poll suggests the public has absorbed that lesson more thoroughly than the Court itself has. We pointed out back in 2024 when polling showed 83% of respondents saying partisanship plays a role in the Court’s decisions at least some of the time.

Only 31% of Republicans — and a mere 7% of Democrats — told Verasight they believe the Court is “just fine the way it is.” So we have an institution beloved by roughly three in 10 members of the party that appointed its current supermajority, and barely one in 14 members of the opposing party. This is what institutional collapse looks like in survey form. We noted just last year that 67% of Republicans and only 26% of Democrats had a favorable view of the Court, and even those numbers showed a deeply troubled institution. Since then, the bottom has continued to fall out.

None of this is happening in a vacuum. A separate Strength In Numbers/Verasight poll earlier this year found 65% of Americans support 18-year term limits for the justices, including 61% of independents and 56% of Republicans. ATL’s own Joe Patrice has been making the case for term limits for years, and his most recent entry in that argument lays out why they represent the least dangerous and most necessary reform on offer. His core argument is that life tenure may have been a shield to insulate the Supreme Court from politics, but it’s a sword now and it’s destroying the Court’s legitimacy. And the momentum for reform is ramping up: Joe has noted that the forces arrayed against reform are getting nervous, a signal that something real is happening.

Listen, Americans — including a substantial number of Republicans — see a Court that has become a political institution, they don’t think that’s acceptable, and they want something done about it. The Verasight poll adds to an already voluminous record, one that Joe has been charting since before it was fashionable, that reform isn’t a fringe demand. It’s the obvious response to an obvious problem.

Earlier: After Years Of ‘Hold My Beer’ Moments, Supreme Court Confidence Hits New Low
Donald Trump Wants Everyone (But Him) To Respect The Supreme Court’s Legitimacy
Americans Know The Truth About The Supreme Court
Fox News Shows Just How Badly Americans Dislike The Supreme Court
Of Course The Supreme Court’s Approval Is In The Tank
The Supreme Court’s Sinking Popularity

Kathryn Rubino is a Senior Editor at Above the Law, host of The Jabot podcast, and co-host of Thinking Like A Lawyer. AtL tipsters are the best, so please connect with her. Feel free to email her with any tips, questions, or comments and follow her on Twitter @Kathryn1 or Bluesky @Kathryn1

The post New Poll Shows Americans — Even Republicans — Think The Supreme Court Needs To Be Reined In appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: The Work Is Waiting: How To Get Involved And Lead Voluntary Bar Associations
articles.content:
每个律师都说想要更广的人脉，但当被邀请加入委员会、需要早到、晚走、打电话、筹款、招募演讲者、追着赞助商、解决没人看到的问题时，就不说话了。这就是律师们从未了解过的律师公会工作。他们只看到招待会、小组讨论、照片、奖项和头衔。看不到那个在最后一刻找到主持人、布置会场、联系餐饮、仍在门口迎接每位嘉宾的志愿者。

如果你想参与自愿性律师公会组织，先从正确的前提开始。这个组织不是为了给你头衔而存在。不是为了给你平台而存在。不是为了填补你营销计划的空白而存在。它的存在是为了服务律师、法官、学生、当事人和公众。从这里出发，一切都会变得更容易。你不再问这个组织能为你做什么，而是开始问需要做什么。

第一步很简单。出现。不是偶尔。不是只在活动光鲜的时候。不是只在认识演讲者的时候。要经常出现，让人们不用你自我介绍就能记住你的名字。参加午餐会、继续教育活动、社交酒会、慈善活动、开放的理事会会议和委员会电话会议。许多律师想在服务之前就当领导。这很少奏效。律师公会，像律师事务所和法庭一样，记住可靠的人。记住那个做好准备并信守承诺的律师。

一旦你出现了，选择一条赛道。太多律师加入五个组织，参加两个活动，志愿加入三个委员会，结果什么都没完成。挑选一两个适合你的执业领域、价值观、社区或人生阶段的团体。年轻律师可以从青年律师部开始。诉讼律师可以加入辩护律师、原告律师、刑事、家庭或破产律师协会。关心司法公正的律师可以加入法律援助或公益团体。最好的赛道不总是最负盛名的赛道，而是你会真正去做工作的赛道。

然后接受那些不光彩的任务。主动负责签到。主动确认演讲者。主动撰写活动描述。主动拉来三个赞助商。主动起草议程。主动打电话给停止参加活动的成员。主动帮助财务人员核对账目。主动坐在没人愿意加入的委员会里。领导力从这里开始。它在远离讲台的地方开始。它从服务开始。

一个实用的开始方式是向现任领导要一个具体的工作任务和截止日期。不要要求一个模糊的帮助机会。问你能否确认参会者名单、起草赞助商信函、准备介绍词、更新网站、收集简历，或处理活动后的跟进。小任务能暴露大习惯。它们显示你是否能沟通、是否完成工作、是否关心细节。当更大的机会出现时，它们也让领导有具体的东西记住你。做好一件明确工作的律师，比那些一直说想更多地参与但不需要掌声或别人不断提醒的律师更容易获得信任。

不要混淆可见度和领导力。有些律师想要麦克风。有些想要社交媒体帖子。有些想要官员晋升之路。有些想要站在法官、管理合伙人或选举官员旁边拍照。可见度有价值，但它不是领导力。领导力是关心那些独自站在房间后面的年轻律师。领导力是准确告诉赞助商组织会提供什么，然后兑现承诺。领导力是把功劳让给别人。

如果你想在自愿性律师公会组织中晋升，成为有用的人。有用的律师会被邀请回来。有用的律师会被邀请进入更小的圈子。有用的律师通过减轻他人的压力来赢得信任。他们不等待完美的指示。他们问正确的问题，完成任务，在别人催促之前主动汇报。一个能为忙碌的领导分担工作的志愿者会变得不可或缺。

尊重那些维持组织运转的人。员工、执行董事、行政人员、财务人员和长期志愿者通常比新来的官员更了解情况。倾听他们的意见。询问以前什么方法有效。询问什么失败了以及为什么。不要带着一个无视历史、预算、章程或关系的宏大计划到来。律师公会靠善意运转，但善意是脆弱的。当你尊重制度知识时，你能避免旧的错误并更快建立信任。当你忽视它时，你只是另一个给每个人制造工作的临时领导。

下一步是在你需要之前建立关系。律师公会领导力不是那种浅薄的社交。它不是收集名片或添加联系人。它是长期的共同工作。你会了解到谁信守承诺。谁能运营一个项目。谁能平复一个房间。谁能筹款。这些关系会成为职业友谊。多年后，当你需要建议、转介绍、演讲者或咨询对象时，与你一起工作过的人会接你的电话，因为你曾经接了他们的电话。

你也需要了解协会的业务。每个自愿性律师公会都有使命，但也有账单要付。场地、餐饮、员工、技术、保险、奖项、助学金、网站、印刷和行政都有成本。好的领导理解财务。好的领导尊重赞助商。好的领导知道免费活动仍然需要有人买单。了解哪些项目盈利、亏损或持平。愿景很重要，但数学决定愿景能否存活。

项目设计是领导力的另一个考验。任何人都能建议一个主题。领导力是建立人们真正会参加的项目。他们询问成员需要什么，而不是领导想谈什么。他们找出真正教学的演讲者，而不是表演者。他们平衡法官、执业律师、公司法务、学者、理赔专业人员、专家和新人律师。他们培养主持人。他们避免那种五个人重复泛泛之谈一小时的懒散项目。一个强有力的律师公会项目应该让律师们带着第二天早上就能用的东西离开。

指导应该是这项工作的核心。最强的自愿性律师公会不只是举办活动。它们创造阶梯。它们把法学生带进房间。它们给年轻律师小角色，然后是更大的角色。它们把新成员介绍给资深律师。它们教授如何写作、演讲、领导和服务。如果你在领导岗位上，你的工作不是占着位子直到下一个人来接班。你的工作是培养下一个人。

这意味着继任计划从第一天就开始了。弱的领导囤积责任，因为被需要感觉很好。强的领导分担责任，因为组织必须超越任何一个人存活。如果你是委员会主席，指定一个副主席。如果你运营一个活动，带上一个人。如果你有赞助商关系，把另一个领导介绍给那个赞助商。当所有知识都只存在于一个人的收件箱时，律师公会就会遭殃。领导力需要制度。

冲突会来的。它总是会来。志愿者们会在钱、演讲者、政治、奖项、日程安排、公开声明、司法介入、赞助和功劳等问题上产生分歧。像专业人士一样处理冲突。先倾听。把问题和这个人分开。在有证据之前假设善意。不要把每个分歧都变成对人格的评判。不要通过八卦来解决问题。最好的律师公会领导能坚定地不同意，清晰地决定，并仍然维护关系。

你还应该保护文化。如果领导不小心，自愿性律师公会可能变成俱乐部。它可能变成一个同样的人发言、赞助、参加和获奖的地方。这可能感觉舒服，但它会慢慢杀死组织。新律师需要入口。多元化的律师需要真正的机会，而不是象征性的邀请。小所律师需要感觉像大所律师一样受欢迎。健康的律师公会不断扩大圈子。

对于想领导的律师来说，路径并不神秘。持续参加。早点志愿。做小工作。加入委员会。主持小组委员会。从头到尾帮助一个活动。拉来一个赞助商。招募五个成员。把新律师介绍给老律师。为简报写稿——主持继续教育课程。学习章程。理解预算。尊重员工。感谢做工作的人。你的记录会为你说话。

奖励不仅仅是头衔。奖励是更大的职业生活。律师公会工作在许多律师在事务所内获得这个机会之前就教会了他们领导力。它教会公开演讲、预算、外交、项目管理、筹款、指导和危机应对。它让你认识你案件压力之外的法官和律师。它给你对职业的更广阔视野。它提醒你法律是一个社区。

所以不要等到你感觉准备好了。你永远不会完全准备好。不要等到有人拍拍你的肩膀。把自己放进房间。询问哪里需要帮助。接受没人想要的任务。做好它。再做一次。自愿性律师公会中的领导力不是印在节目单上的头衔授予的。它是在节目开始前、在房间空无一人之后、在照片发布很久之后，在安静的工作中赢得的。工作正在等待。

弗兰克·拉莫斯是迈阿密Goldberg Segalla律师事务所的合伙人，从事商业诉讼、产品和重大人身伤害业务。你可以在领英上关注他，他有大约8万名粉丝。

这篇文章《工作正在等待：如何参与并领导自愿性律师公会组织》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Every lawyer says they want a bigger network until someone asks them to join a committee, show up early, stay late, make calls, raise money, recruit speakers, chase sponsors, and solve problems no one sees. That is the part of bar work most lawyers never hear about. They see the reception, the panel, the photograph, the award, and the title. They do not see the volunteer who found the last-minute moderator, fixed the room setup, called the caterer, and still welcomed every guest at the door.

If you want to get involved in a voluntary bar association, start with the right premise. The association does not exist to give you a title. It does not exist to give you a platform. It does not exist to fill the gaps in your marketing plan. It exists to serve lawyers, judges, students, clients, and the public. When you begin there, everything becomes easier. You stop asking what the organization can do for you. You start asking what needs to be done.

The first step is simple. Show up. Not once. Not when the event is glamorous. Not only when you know the speaker. Show up often enough that people learn your name without you having to announce it. Attend the lunches, CLEs, happy hours, charity events, open board meetings, and committee calls. Many lawyers want to lead before they have served. That rarely works. Bar associations, like law firms and courtrooms, remember reliability. They remember the lawyer who comes prepared and follows through.

Once you show up, choose a lane. Too many lawyers join five organizations, attend two events, volunteer for three committees, and accomplish nothing. Pick one or two groups that fit your practice, values, community, or stage of life. A young lawyer may start with a young lawyers’division. A trial lawyer may join a defense, plaintiffs, criminal, family, or bankruptcy bar. A lawyer who cares about access to justice may join a legal aid or pro bono group. The best lane is not always the most prestigious one. It is the one where you will do the work.

Then take the unglamorous assignment. Offer to handle registration. Offer to confirm speakers. Offer to write the event description. Offer to bring in three sponsors. Offer to draft the agenda. Offer to call members who have stopped attending. Offer to help the treasurer reconcile a problem. Offer to sit on the committee, but no one joins because it sounds boring. That is where leadership starts. It starts far from the podium. It starts with service.

One practical way to start is to ask a current leader for one discrete job with a deadline. Do not ask for a vague chance to help. Ask whether you can confirm attendee lists, draft a sponsor letter, prepare introductions, update the website, collect biographies, or handle follow-up after the event. Small assignments reveal big habits. They show whether you communicate, finish, and care about details. They also give leaders something concrete to remember when larger roles open. The lawyer who did one clear job well is easier to trust than the lawyer who kept saying they wanted to get more involved, without needing applause or constant reminders from others.

Do not confuse visibility with leadership. Some lawyers want the microphone. Some want the social media post. Some want the officer track. Some want to be seen standing next to the judge, the managing partner, or the elected official. Visibility has value, but it is not leadership. Leadership is checking on the young lawyer standing alone at the back of the room. Leadership is telling a sponsor exactly what the organization will deliver and then delivering it. Leadership is giving credit away.

If you want to rise in a voluntary bar association, become useful. Useful lawyers get asked back. Useful lawyers get invited into smaller rooms. Useful lawyers earn trust by reducing everyone else’s stress. They do not wait for perfect instructions. They ask the right questions, do the assignment, and report back before anyone chases them. A volunteer who lightens the load for busy leaders becomes indispensable.

Respect the people who keep the association alive. Staff, executive directors, administrators, treasurers, and long-time volunteers often know more than the incoming officers. Listen to them. Ask what has worked before. Ask what failed and why. Do not arrive with a grand plan that ignores history, budget, bylaws, or relationships. Bar associations run on goodwill, but goodwill is fragile. When you respect institutional knowledge, you avoid old mistakes and build trust faster. When you dismiss it, you make yourself another temporary leader who creates work for everyone else.

The next step is to build relationships before you need them. Bar leadership is not networking in the shallow sense. It is not collecting cards or adding contacts. It is shared work over time. You learn who keeps promises. You learn who can run a program. You learn who can calm a room. You learn who can raise money. Those relationships become professional friendships. Years later, when you need advice, a referral, a speaker, or a sounding board, the people you served with will answer your call because you answered theirs.

You also need to learn about the association’s business. Every voluntary bar has a mission, but it also has bills to pay. There are costs for venues, meals, staff, technology, insurance, awards, scholarships, websites, printing, and administration. A good leader understands finances. A good leader respects sponsors. A good leader knows that free events still cost someone money. Learn what programs make money, lose money, or break even. Vision matters, but math decides whether the vision survives.

Programming is another test of leadership. Anyone cansuggest a topic. Leaders build programs that people attend. They ask what members need, not what the leader wants to talk about. They identify speakers who teach rather than perform. They balance judges, practitioners, in-house counsel, academics, claims professionals, experts, and newer lawyers. They prepare moderators. They avoid the lazy program where five people repeat generalities for an hour. A strong bar program should leave lawyers with something they can use the next morning.

Mentorship should be at the center of this work. The strongest voluntary bars do not simply host events. They create ladders. They bring law students into the room. They give young lawyers small roles, then bigger ones. They introduce new members to senior lawyers. They teach how to write, speak, lead, and serve. If you are in leadership, your job is not to occupy the chair until the next person takes it. Your job is to build the next person.

That means succession planning starts on day one. A weak leader hoards responsibility because being needed feels good. A strong leader shares responsibility because the organization must outlive any one person. If you chair a committee, identify a vice chair. If you run an event, bring someone with you. If you have a sponsor relationship, introduce another leader to that sponsor. Voluntary bars suffer when all the knowledge lives in one person’s inbox. Leadership requires systems.

Conflict will come. It always does. Volunteers disagree about money, speakers, politics, awards, scheduling, public statements, judicial involvement, sponsorship, and credit. Handle conflict like a professional. Listen first. Separate the issue from the person. Assume good faith until you have proof otherwise. Do not turn every disagreement into a character judgment. Do not gossip your way through a problem. The best bar leaders can disagree firmly, decide clearly, and still preserve relationships.

You should also protect the culture. A voluntary bar can become a clubhouse if leaders are not careful. It can become a place where the same people speak, sponsor, attend, and receive awards. That may feel comfortable, but it slowly kills the organization. New lawyers need a door in. Diverse lawyers need real opportunities, not token invitations. Smaller-firm lawyers need to feel as welcome as big-firm lawyers. A healthy bar association keeps widening the circle.

For lawyers who want to lead, the path is not mysterious. Attend consistently. Volunteer early. Do the small jobs well. Join a committee. Chair a subcommittee. Help with one event from beginning to end. Bring in a sponsor. Recruit five members. Introduce new lawyers to old members. Write for the newsletter—moderate a CLE. Learn the bylaws. Understand the budget. Respect the staff. Thank the people who do the work. Your record will speak for you.

The reward is not just a title. The reward is a larger professional life. Bar work teaches leadership before many lawyers get that chance inside their firms. It teaches public speaking, budgeting, diplomacy, project management, fundraising, mentoring, and crisis response. It introduces you to judges and lawyers outside the pressure of a case. It gives you a broader view of the profession. It reminds you that the law is a community.

So do not wait until you feel ready. You will never feel fully ready. Do not wait until someone taps you on the shoulder. Put yourself in the room. Ask where help is needed. Take the assignment no one else wants. Do it well. Do it again. Leadership in voluntary bar associations is not granted by a title printed on a program. It is earned in the quiet work before the program starts, after the room empties, and long after the photograph is posted. The work is waiting.

Frank Ramos is a partner at Goldberg Segalla in Miami, where he practices commercial litigation, products, and catastrophic personal injury. You can follow him on LinkedIn, where he has about 80,000 followers.

The post The Work Is Waiting: How To Get Involved And Lead Voluntary Bar Associations appeared first on Above the Law.
